welcome: Welcome, family. This is a living exhibit of where our shared history stands — the people, the places they came from, the journeys they made, and the small human details worth passing around the table.

hero.title: Bread for a royal household, and a village that vanished
hero.body: On our German side, the Zarges family lived on the grounds of a Hohenzollern royal estate near a village called Basdorf. The grandfather tended sheep and baked bread in a great outdoor oven for the imperial household. Then the almost fairy-tale ending: after the family sailed for America, Basdorf was flooded to build a reservoir — the village they left behind now rests underwater.
hero.image_caption: A Zarges family photograph would be perfect here.

journey.title: From a Baltic port to a Pennsylvania coal town
journey.body: In the spring of 1910, John Romejko Sr. left the parish country northeast of Vilnius, boarded the steamship Lituania at the Baltic port of Libau, and crossed the Atlantic to New York — reaching Ellis Island on 30 March. From there the family moved inland to the anthracite coal town of Shenandoah, Pennsylvania.

route.note: a new life in coal country

timeline.label: John Stranford born in County Londonderry
John Romejko Sr. born near Vilnius
John Sr. becomes a U.S. citizen
Joseph B. Romako born in Shenandoah

facts.title: The trail reaches Ulster
facts.body: The furthest-back person we can stand behind is John Stranford, born March 1795 in County Londonderry, Ireland — an Ulster immigrant who died in 1855 in Pennsylvania, and your fourth-great-grandfather. His own parents, born around 1770, are already on the tree.

facts.title: Sworn in by the saloon keeper
facts.body: When John Romejko Sr. became a U.S. citizen in the coal town of Shenandoah in 1916, the two men who vouched for him weren't lawyers — they were neighbors. One, John Dzienis, ran a saloon on South Main Street. In an immigrant coal town, the corner tavern was the heart of the community.

facts.title: Two brothers register for war
facts.body: Wartime draft cards surfaced for John Aloysius Romako (born 1914) and Joseph Bernard Romako (born 1922), both born in Shenandoah. The cards even record their height and build — small, human snapshots of who these men were on the day they signed.

facts.title: A possible noble clan
facts.body: One promising — still unproven — lead points the Korsak name toward the Korsak szlachta, the old Polish-Lithuanian nobility, listed in Boniecki's classic armorial of Polish gentry. Nothing confirmed yet, but exactly the kind of thread that makes you keep digging.

region.name: Buivydžiai, Lithuania
region.blurb: The Romejko and Korsack lines trace back toward this little parish town (Polish: Bujwidze), northeast of Vilnius, in what was then the Russian Empire. We are searching its 19th-century church registers page by page for the family's baptisms.
region.image_caption: A page from the 1890 Buivydžiai parish baptism register (Lithuanian State Historical Archives, Fund 1399) — the kind of record we read line by line, in Cyrillic script.
